title: Couples sit next to each other

content.problemStatement:
There are **N** couples numbered 1 through **N**. Each couple consists of two people. There are also **2N** chairs numbered 1 through **2N**. Each chair has a person sitting on it; for each **i** (1 ≤ **i** ≤ **2N**), the person initially sitting on chair **i** belongs to couple number **Ai**. The chairs are placed in a circle, so chairs **i** and **i+1** are adjacent for each 1 ≤ **i** ≤ **2N-1**; chairs **2N** and **1** are also adjacent.

You may perform the following operation any number of times (including zero): choose two people sitting on adjacent chairs and swap them.

We would like all couples to sit on adjacent chairs. Compute the minimum number of operations needed to achieve this.
constraints: 1 <= sum of N over all test cases <= 1000000
1 <= Ai <= N for each valid i
each number from 1 to N appears in A exactly twice
content.inputFormat: The first line contains a single integer T denoting the number of test cases. The first line of each test case contains a single integer N. The second line contains 2N space-separated integers A1, A2, ..., A2N.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print a single line containing one number — the minimum number of operations required to make each couple sit next to each other.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For the first test case, we can swap the second person with the third person to get the desired arrangement. For the second test case, we can swap the second person with the fourth person and then swap the third person with the fifth person to get the desired arrangement.
We can swap the third person with the fourth person and then swap the fifth person with the sixth person to get the desired arrangement.
The couple is already sitting next to each other, so no swaps are needed.

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we can use a greedy approach. We iterate over the array and whenever we find a couple that is not sitting next to each other, we swap the person with the next person in the array until we find the other person of the couple. We keep track of the number of swaps made and return it as the minimum number of operations required.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * N) — where T is the number of test cases and N is the number of couples. This is because in the worst case, we might have to iterate over the entire array for each test case.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) — we only use a constant amount of space to store the number of swaps and the current index.

code.Python3:
import sys
input = sys.stdin.readline
T = int(input())
for _ in range(T):
    N = int(input())
    A = list(map(int, input().split()))
    swaps = 0
    for i in range(2 * N - 1):
        if A[i] != A[i + 1]:
            j = i + 1
            while A[i] != A[j]:
                j += 1
            while j > i + 1:
                A[j], A[j - 1] = A[j - 1], A[j]
                j -= 1
                swaps += 1
    print(swaps)
